observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our audit recommendations that the Municipal
Accountant (i) provide sufficient and appropriate details and supporting
documents for all reconciling items in the BRS with emphasis on those
inadequately labelled items to ensure their propriety and thereafter make the
necessary adjustments in the books; and (ii) take up in the books the valid book
reconciling items duly substantiated with appropriate documentation (e.g.
copies of debit and credit memos).

Management’s Comment
During the Exit Conference, the Municipal Accountant informed the Audit
Team that a follow-up communication had already been sent to LBP – Coron
Branch, requesting the necessary details and supporting documents for the
outstanding credit and debit memos related to prior years, which remain
unreconciled. With regards to the reconciling items for CY 2024, the Municipal
Accountant committed to promptly initiate a formal request to the bank to obtain
the pertinent documentation. Furthermore, the management assured the Audit
Team of its commitment to take the necessary actions to complete the reconciliation
and proper recording of these transactions. It also pledged to institute measures that
will promote the regular and timely preparation of bank reconciliations moving
forward, in adherence to sound accounting principles and best practices.

b. Unadjusted stale checks totaling ₱61,327.70 contrary to Section 59 of MNGAS
for LGUs, Volume I
Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, states that checks may be
cancelled when they become spoiled or stale. A check is considered stale, if it has
been outstanding for over six months from date of issue or as prescribed by the
depository bank. At least one month before a check becomes stale, the Treasurer
shall send a written notice to the payee of the existence of the check.
Moreover, a check that is considered spoiled or stale shall be marked
cancelled on its face and reported as follows:
 For stale checks which have been unclaimed, and thus the original DV and
supporting documents are still with the Treasurer, the cancelled check shall
be presented in the RCI after the last check issued for the period indicated
in the report. The original DV and supporting documents shall be returned
t o t h e accountant who shall prepare a JEV to record the transaction as
Accounts Payable.
 For checks which became spoiled or stale in the hands o f the payee and
which require replacement, a new check may be issued upon submission of
the spoiled or stale check t o the Treasurer. A certified copy o f the DV shall
be requested from the Auditor for presentation t o the Administrator/Local
Chief Executive who shall countersign the check. The cancelled check shall
be reported and attached t o the RCI prepared at the period of cancellation.
The replacement check shall also be reported chronologically in the RCI.
A review of stale checks reported in the prior year’s audit revealed that most
of these had already been adjusted in the accounting records in accordance with the
Audit Team’s recommendations. However, it was noted that three (3) checks issued
in 2024, along with one (1) check from 2023 which had previously remained
unadjusted, have become stale but remain unadjusted and still appear as outstanding
items in the Bank Reconciliation Statement (BRS) as of year-end.

The checks presented under Table ‘1.3’ had already been issued and
received by the respective payees but were neither deposited nor encashed with
their depository banks. The failure to cancel these stale checks and their continued
inclusion as outstanding items in the BRS resulted in the understatement of both
the LCCA balance and the corresponding payable accounts reported in the
Statement of Financial Position.
We reiterated our recommendations that the:
a. Municipal Treasurer (i) locate the payees of the stale checks and inform
them of the status of their checks so that appropriate action may be
taken to adjust the accounts accordingly; and (ii) send written notice to
the concerned payees at least one month before a check becomes stale
and observe the proper cancellation and reporting of stale check in
compliance with Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
b. Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments in the books for the
long outstanding/stale checks to correct the Cash in Bank-LCCA and
payable account balances.

Management’s Comment
In the written reply received on May 16, 2025, the Municipal Treasurer
reported that one of the four payees had already been located and duly notified, and
that appropriate steps were being undertaken to comply with the prescribed
procedures for the cancellation and reporting of stale checks pertaining to the
remaining payees.
Subsequently, during the Exit Conference, both the Municipal Treasurer
and the Municipal Accountant confirmed that all identified stale checks had since
been officially cancelled and that the necessary adjusting entries had been recorded
in the books of accounts. They acknowledged the audit observations and assured
the Audit Team of their commitment to implement the recommendations without
delay. Moreover, they pledged to adopt more proactive measures to prevent the
recurrence of similar issues in the future.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Accountant (i) continue sending regular written notices to all AOs
concerned at least quarterly to demand the settlement of their outstanding
cash advances; (ii) ensure that no new cash advance is granted unless previous
ones have been fully liquidated; and
b. Local Chief Executive and the Municipal Accountant, strictly enforce the
provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002, by requiring all AOs to fully liquidate
their cash advances within the prescribed period and return any unexpended
balances immediately.

Management’s Comment
During the Exit Conference, the Municipal Accountant committed to continuously
send demand letters to accountable officers with outstanding balances and to consider the
withholding of salaries as a measure to compel liquidation. Management also expressed
their full commitment to the audit recommendation and assured that efforts will be made
in order to minimize the balance of outstanding cash advances.

We recommended that the:
a. Inventory Committee regularly conduct physical count of inventories and
prepare the corresponding RPCI in compliance with existing rules.
b. Accounting Department:
i. review all Inventory accounts and reconcile them with the actual items on
hand and those already issued; and
ii. adjust the inventory account and recognize expense for issued inventories.
c. Management:
i. Establish internal controls to ensure that inventory issuance and
accounting are properly coordinated between the General Services Office
(GSO)/Supply Officer, Accounting, and End-User Units and that the
required forms, registry, and reports are being used appropriately; and
ii. Strictly implement the provisions of COA Circular No. 2022-004, ensuring
that inventory transactions are accounted for accurately and in a timely
manner.

Management’s Comment
During the Exit Conference with management, the Chairman of the Inventory
Committee acknowledged the audit observations and assured the Audit Team of the
Committee’s commitment to the stricter enforcement of established procedures governing
the receipt and issuance of inventory items moving forward. He further stated that priority
will be given to the conduct of a comprehensive physical inventory count and the
reconciliation of inventory records to ensure accuracy and reliability. Additionally, he
committed to the full implementation of the audit recommendations and adherence to the
prescribed accounting guidelines for the proper recording and management of inventory
transactions.

We recommended that the:
1. Management:
a. Strictly adhere to the prescribed accounting and property management
policies under the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and COA Circular No.
2015-008, by:
i. Maintaining complete and updated Property, Plant and Equipment
Ledger Cards (PPELCs), Real Property Ledger Cards (RPLCs),
Local Road Network Ledger Cards (LRNLCs), and Property Cards
(PCs); and
ii. Ensuring that all asset acquisitions and disposals are properly
recorded, supported with relevant documents, and updated in the
subsidiary ledgers.
b. Conduct a thorough reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the Accounting
and GSO records, to identify and resolve discrepancies in the reported
asset balances, condition (i.e., serviceable vs. unserviceable), and location;
c. Exclude unserviceable and missing assets from the reported inventory, and
take appropriate action for their disposition and derecognition, to ensure
that the PPE account reflects only existing and serviceable items in
accordance with applicable laws and regulations;
d. Institute internal controls and coordination mechanisms between the GSO
and the Accounting Office to ensure consistency, accuracy, and integrity
of PPE records moving forward;
e. Provide capacity-building or refresher training on proper property and
accounting records management to enhance compliance and minimize
recurrence of these deficiencies;
f. Avail the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts in compliance with the
guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31,
2020, which includes:
i. The preparation and submission of PIP;
ii. The conduct of a comprehensive physical inventory of PPE items;
iii. Reconciliation of inventory results (per RPCPPE) with both the
property and accounting records;
iv. Proper disposition of non-existing, missing, or unserviceable items in
accordance with applicable rules and procedures;
v. Derecognition of PPE items without available record of
accountability, as warranted.
2. Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to reclassify the remaining
tangible items with cost below ₱50,000.00 and previously classified as PPE to
Prior Period Adjustment and Government Equity accounts and close the
corresponding accumulated depreciation in the books of accounts, if already
issued to the end-user, pursuant to the provisions under COA Circular Nos.
2022-004 and 2024-006 so as to effect the retrospective application of the new
accounting policy and correct the PPE and other related balances; and
3. Supply and/or Property and Accounting Divisions/Units maintain the
appropriate forms, registry, and reports as prescribed under the Circulars to
strengthen internal controls over the semi-expendable property.

Management’s Comment
During the Exit Conference, the Head of the Inventory Committee acknowledged
the audit observation and committed to continue the implementation of the One-Time
Cleansing of the PPE accounts, targeted to resume this July 2025. He further stated that the
Committee plans to request the disposal of unserviceable items and investigate the missing
items recorded in the books. Additionally, he mentioned the plan to explore a stand-alone
system for the tracking and monitoring of all PPE items to ensure consistent and reliable
recordkeeping.
The Municipal Accountant likewise expressed commitment in implementing the
recommended adjustments, particularly the reclassification of the remaining tangible items
to their appropriate asset accounts. He also affirmed the municipality’s continued use of
the prescribed forms and compliance with the relevant accounting and auditing rules and
regulations.

We recommended that Management:
a. Adjust the affected accounts to reflect the correct recording of loan principal
payments as deductions from Loans Payable;
b. Review prior periods for similar misstatements and make necessary
adjustments;
c. Strengthen internal controls over journal entries and ensure that accounting
personnel are fully guided by applicable IPSAS and COA rules; and
d. Conduct capacity-building activities or refresher training for accounting staff
on proper accounting treatment of loan transactions.

Management’s Comment
During the Exit Conference, the Municipal Accountant acknowledged the improper
recording of loan amortization payments in the books and concurred with the audit
observation. He also committed to adopt the proposed adjusting journal entries
recommended by the Audit Team and ensure the proper recording of future loan-related
transactions in accordance with applicable accounting standards and guidelines.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Accountant (a) provide sufficient and appropriate details and supporting documents for all reconciling items in the BRS with emphasis on those inadequately labelled items in order to ensure their propriety and thereafter make the necessary adjustments in the books; and (b) make the necessary adjustments in the books for long outstanding/stale checks to correct the CIB-LCCA and payables account balances; and
Municipal Treasurer send written notice to the concerned payees at least one month before a check becomes stale and observe the proper cancellation and reporting of stale check in compliance with Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer (a) prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant at the start of the year a duly certified list showing the taxpayers’ name and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis for setting-up RPT/SET Receivables; and (b) provide the Municipal Accountant with a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies, the format of which is provided in LTO Form 90, as the Municipal Accountant’s basis in taking up in the books the unrecorded tax delinquencies; and
Municipal Accountant adopt the accrual basis of accounting in recognizing RPT and SET Receivables by ensuring the proper setting up of RPT/SET Receivables and their corresponding reciprocal accounts at the beginning of each year based on the Municipal Treasurer’s duly certified list of real property taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor, as head of the agency (a) ensure that cash advances are only granted with specific legal purpose and liquidated/refunded in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002; (b) refrain from granting cash advances for procurement and ensure that the procurement activities are properly planned and are responsive to the Municipality’s operational requirements; and (c) refrain from drawing cash advances other than for his official traveling expenses and those expressly authorized by existing rules and regulations;
Municipal Accountant (a) appropriately classify the grant of cash advances in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs; (b) provide complete information in the RUCA and present every AO’s cash advance and liquidation separately for effective monitoring of the same; and (c) thoroughly review the liquidation reports before certifying the completeness and propriety of supporting documents; and
Municipal Treasurer, through the Municipal Accountant, submit the lacking documentary requirements for the liquidation of his cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, create an Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all its property, plant and equipment to determine the existence and condition of the PPE and submit to the Audit Team the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment duly reconciled with the accounting and property records. Furthermore, in relation to the recently issued COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, direct the Inventory Committee to pursue a one-time cleansing of the Municipality’s PPE account balances following the guidelines laid down in the said COA Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) fully disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the details of the Loans Payable-Domestic account pursuant to paragraph 127 of IPSAS No. 1; (b) prepare an adjusting entry to reclassify the recording of the loan from the Due to NGAs account to Loans Payable-Domestic account; (c) submit the disbursement voucher and supporting documents for the check payment amounting to ₱2,626,453.36 and  ensure strict adherence to Section 7.2.1(a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006 on the prompt rendition of accounts; (d) prepare a journal entry to capitalize the amounts of interest previously paid and ensure that subsequent payments thereof are recognized as PPE and not as expenses; and (e) create a special account in the GF for the loan availed by the LGU for the Construction of Water System in Coron, Palawan. Relative thereto, make the necessary adjustments to transfer the balance of the loan and the total costs incurred to date recorded under the CIP-Infrastructure Assets account from the TF to the GF books.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor, as head of the agency, (a) ensure that payments are made directly to suppliers through checks pursuant to Section 93 of P.D. 1445; and (b) direct the BAC to judiciously plan and carry out the Municipality’s procurement activities to meet its operational requirements to lessen payments for procurement of goods and services thru reimbursements;
Municipal Accountant ensure that disbursement vouchers are supported with complete documentation before certifying the same for payment; and
Municipal Treasurer, submit the proof of payments for the purchases of goods totalling ₱184,609.10.

We recommended that the:

We recommended that:

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE (i) through the Municipal Accountant, serve written notices to all concerned accountable officers requiring immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances and institute legal action against them, if warranted; and (ii) ensure that all concerned officers and employees strictly comply with the guidelines of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to forestall accumulation of unliquidated balances.

observations.managementAction: The SCBAA and NTFS for CY 2020 were submitted to the Audit Team.

No. 9184; and (c) remind the concerned officials that they can be held personally liable for the amount for failing to take necessary measures to ensure that the project is implemented in accordance with the approved plans and schedules.
observations.reason: Liquidated damages are yet to be imposed and collected due to lack of initiative to enforce Section 8, Annex “E” of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer (a) provide the Municipal Accountant with a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies, the format of which is provided in LTO Form 90, as the Municipal Accountant’s basis in taking up in the books the unrecorded tax delinquencies; and (b) submit to the Municipal Accountant at the start of the year a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis for setting-up RPT/SET Receivables; and
Municipal Accountant (a) adopt the accrual basis of accounting in recognizing RPT and SET Receivables; and (b) ensure the proper setting up of RPT/SET Receivables and their corresponding reciprocal accounts at the beginning of the year based on Treasury Office’s duly certified list of real property taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible.
The Municipal Accountant has already reviewed the guidelines in setting up the receivable accounts and shall implement the audit recommendations pertaining to the accrual of the RPT and SET Receivables beginning CY 2021, according to the management's written reply to the Audit Team. Furthermore, the report on RPT and SET deliquencies for CY 2020 will be submitted to the Municipal Accountant's Office for recording purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect the necessary adjustments in the books to correct the understatement of the PPE and Government Equity account balances. Henceforth, ensure that the costs of major repairs/ rehabilitation and construction of property, plant and equipment are capitalized or recognized as PPE in accordance with IPSAS No. 17.
In August 2021, the Municipal Accountant prepared corrective journal entries to record the cost of unrecognized PPE in the books of accounts, in accordance with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the:
Inventory Committee prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System with complete details and cost segregation and the description for road components for road projects; and
Municipal Accountant comply with the disclosure requirements for the total Road Network System in the NTFS by providing essential information to fully understand the account balance.
According to the management's written response to the Audit Team, the Municipal Engineer had already prepared the Inventory Report. However, no inventory report prepared in compliance with the stipulated format under COA Circular No. 2015-008 was submitted as part of the supporting documents to the written responses received by the Audit Team on August 24, 2021. Instead, POWs for road construction projects were presented, along with other papers (such as photos and inspection reports). Concerning the non-compliance with the disclosure requirements in the NTFS, the Municipal Accountant committed to submit a revised NTFS.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer (a) reconcile monthly their records on allotments and obligations, verify resulting discrepancies and submit the required budget reports and reconciliation statement within the prescribed period; and (b) exercise due care in preparing their respective reports to forestall discrepancies and ensure the completeness and accuracy of the information contained therein.
According to the management's written response to the Audit Team, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer have already discussed the procedures in the preparation of the SCBAA and have agreed to diligently observe the regular monthly reconciliation and submission thereof to the Audit Team for audit purposes.

We recommended that the:
LCE direct the Local Finance Committee to prepare and submit the BGCM Report of Fund Utilization pursuant to Section 4.2 of LBC No. 125;
Municipal Accountant (a) prepare and submit the year-end trial balance and financial statements for the SAGF-BGCM; and (b) carefully review the disbursements to ensure the completeness and regularity of supporting documents pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445; and
Municipality, through its BAC, strictly comply with the mandatory posting to the GPPB Online Portal of information relative to its emergency procurements under the Bayanihan Act for transparency purposes.
In its written response to the Audit Team, Management committed to implement the audit recommendations. Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant stated that transaction documents related to the use of the BGCM were filed separately for easier retrieval and subsequent reporting.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
Although separate filing of transaction documents for the BGCM would be useful in simple retrieval of records when needed, such as for the preparation of utilization reports, a separate special account in the general fund for the BGCM should also have been kept in line with Section 3.7 of LBC No. 125 of April 7, 2020.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor ensure compliance with the guidelines set by the DSWD and other government agencies in the proper documentation of the distribution of similar type of financial assistance coursed through the Municipality;
Special Disbursing Officer, together with the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking payrolls to substantiate the liquidations; and
MSWDO, together with the Municipal Accountant, submit the lacking SACs which must contain the important details for verification by the Audit Team as well as the other lacking documentary requirements.
Based on the management's written response to the Audit Team, the MSWDO exerted extra efforts to comply with the DSWD's SAP guidelines. Concerning the unsubmitted payrolls, it is possible that these were erroneously included in the copies submitted to the DSWD Regional Office. In the meantime, the Treasury Office's file copies have been photocopied and submitted to the Audit Team. Management promised that the original copies would be turned over to the Audit Team once they are recovered.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor ensure utmost compliance with the provisions of Section 93 of P.D. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002 which require that payments must be made directly to suppliers and strictly adhere with the provisions of R.A. 9184 on the appropriate method of procurement of goods and services;
Municipal Accountant (a) ensure the completeness of supporting documents before processing and certifying the DVs; (b) make the necessary adjustments to correct the erroneous recording of the purchased equipment amounting to ₱97,600.00; and (c) compute withholding taxes from suppliers in accordance with withholding tax laws, rules and regulations and other related issuance of the BIR; and
Municipal Treasurer submit the proof of payments for purchases of goods and services totaling ₱355,161.00.
Management responded that strict implementation of the audit recommendations will begin immediately. In response to the audit finding on the failure to capitalize the cost of security lights, a correcting entry was already prepared by the Municipal Accountant to recognize the assets.

We recommended that the BAC Chairperson, through the BAC Secretariat (a) submit a copy of all contracts and purchase orders executed in CY 2020 either in printed or electronic form, whichever is more convenient or cost-efficient; and (b) henceforth, observe regular submission of contracts and purchase orders to the Audit Team in such manner and timing as may be deemed reasonable in view of the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001.
According to the BAC Chairperson’s written response to the Audit Team, which was received on August 24, 2021, regular submission of contracts and purchase orders shall be dutifully observed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to dutifully observe the prompt preparation and submission of monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements to the Audit Team for verification to ascertain the correctness of the Cash in Bank account balance in the financial statements in compliance with Section 3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

discontinue the practice of allowing PS and MOOE items to be charged against the
continuing appropriations; and (b) cause the reversion of unappropriated PS and MOOE
to the current appropriations through an Appropriation Ordinance or Supplemental
Budget to be enacted by Sangguniang Bayan.

We recommended that the:
1. LCE:
a. thru the Municipal Accountant, BAC and BAC Secretariat, (i) cause the
immediate submission of the lacking documents; (ii) require the concerned
officials to render satisfactory explanation for the deficiencies noted in audit;
and (iii) henceforth, ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA
Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR.; and
b. take appropriate action to collect the liquidated damages amounting to
₱257,899.26 from the suppliers who incurred delays in deliveries and ensure
that the terms and conditions indicated in the purchase orders and contracts
are strictly complied with.
2. BAC strictly abide by the standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its Revised IRR and
other relevant laws on government procurement; and
3. Municipal Accountant ensure completeness of supporting documents before
processing and certifying the DVs.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and the
BAC Secretariat (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents which
are necessary in the technical evaluation and validation of the projects; and (ii)
henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001, R.A. No.
9184 and its Revised I.R.R. on the procurement and implementation of infrastructure
projects.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure submission of the
Certificate of Final Acceptance by the Municipal Engineer and posting of Warranty
Security by the contractor before the retention money and performance security are
released to the latter.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Engineer and the MPDC (i) reevaluate and complete the list of
infrastructure projects in the monitoring report and provide all the minimum
required information on the implementation of each project; and (ii) immediately
submit to the Audit Team the consolidated status report/information of all the
infrastructure projects started, completed and still on-going as of December 31,
2018;
b. Municipal Engineer hasten the implementation of 28 projects and make sure that
all infrastructure projects are strictly monitored and implemented in accordance
with plans and schedules so that the intended beneficiaries could enjoy the benefits
of these programs;
c. LCE, ME and MPDC strictly comply with the guidelines of COA Circular No.
2013-004 regarding information and publicity of PPAs as well as the general
provisions of succeeding GAAs on transparency in the status of implementation
of PPAs;
d. LCE (i) issue termination of contract to erring contractors who have negative
slippage of ten percent (10%) or more in the completion of the work pursuant to
Section III-A(2) of the Guidelines on Termination of Contracts; and (ii) cause the
forfeiture of their performance security pursuant to Section 8.5 of the R-IRR of
R.A. No. 9184; and
e. Municipal Accountant and ME compute and impose liquidated damages against
the six contractors of the 13 infrastructure projects which were completed beyond
contract expiry date pursuant to Section 8 of the Contract Implementation
Guidelines for the Procurement of Infrastructure Projects.

We recommended that the:
1. LCE, Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator and Local Development Council (a) strictly comply with the
provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 to ensure that all the PPAs charged
against the 20% Development Fund are proper to attain desirable socio-economic
and environmental management outcomes of LGU Coron; and (b) ensure that
projects, programs and activities included in the AIP contain description, location
and schedule of implementation for easy achievement of specific objectives.
2. Municipal Treasurer transfer promptly the 20% component of IRA to the 20%DF
depository bank account, including the remaining amount which was retained in
the LBP-IRA account as of December 31, 2018; and
3. Municipal Accountant ensure that charges in the 20%DF do not include personal
services and MOOE or those expenditures considered non-investment or non-
capital in nature pursuant to Item 2.3 of the same JMC.

We recommended that the:
1. Municipal Engineer and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) provide
justification/proof that the two road concreting projects are distinct and no
duplication of projects nor overlapping of funding had occurred;
2. Members of the Sangguniang Bayan ensure that all changes in the annual budget
such as re-appropriation of savings or realignment of projects or abandonment of
PPAs to any item of expenditure under any class or to any new item of expenditure
not included in the annual budget are covered by an appropriation ordinance or
supplemental budget and submit the same to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan for
review;
3. Municipal Budget Officer certify the existence of valid appropriations only for
expenditures supported with supplemental budget or appropriation ordinance
duly authorized by the Sanggunian;
4. Local Chief Executive compel the Municipal Engineer to submit the road
maps/layouts, technical specifications, detailed estimates of the POW, as-built
plans for the completed project/s, properly labeled photographs taken before,
during and after construction of the project (with exact location coordinates) and
the lacking supporting documents for each project mentioned in previous AOM
No. Co2019-21 dated July 23, 2018 as these documents are necessary in the
technical evaluation and inspection or validation of work accomplished for both
projects; and
5. Municipal Engineer (a) prepare the detailed engineering design and POW in
accordance with the DPWH issuances as well as the guidelines issued by the source
agencies of fund transferred to the LGU; and (b) geotag pictures in every stages
of infrastructure projects and provide the exact location of projects such as
coordinate and station especially for road network projects for purposes of
transparency and accurate reporting of project accomplishments.

We recommended that the:
1. Local Chief Executive, as the head of the agency and the LDRRM Council (a)
ensure that the portion of the LGU’s total LDRRMF pertaining to the 5%
LDRRMF (30% Quick Response Fund and 70% Mitigation Fund) and
unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the preceding years within the five-year
validity period of the STF are duly supported with the LDRRMFIP;
2. Municipal Accountant (a) maintain SLs for the TL– DRRM account under the TF
books for the transfers of the LGU’s unexpended DRRMF to the STF by year of
transfer, receipts of NDRRMF by transferring agency and DRRMF from other
LGUs and donors; (b) recognize the appropriate adjustments in the TF books to
properly recognize the TL– DRRMF account for the receipts of amounts held in
trust for the DRRMF; and (c) support the transfers of unexpended/unobligated
balances with schedules such as the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of
the DRRMF;
3. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer reconcile the amount of
unexpended balances per accounting reports and per budgetary reports in order
to correctly determine the amount of unexpended balances of current and
previous years’ unexpended QRF and DRRMF– MOOE to be transferred to the
STF; and
4. Municipal Accountant and Treasurer transfer the unexpended balance of the
LDRRMF to the special trust fund pursuant to Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121 to
ensure that funds are available for disaster risk reduction and management
activities.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. demand payment from E.M. Abin Trading and Construction for the liquidated
damages equivalent to 86 days delay on the completion of the project, otherwise,
concerned officials can be held personally liable for the amount for failing to take
necessary measures to ensure that the project is implemented in accordance with
the approved plans and schedules;
b. thru the MDRRM Officer and the Municipal Engineer, ensure strict compliance
to the basic facilities and standards required for an evacuation center in
accordance with the guidelines set forth in DEPED-DSWD-DILG-DOH JMC No.
1, s. 2013; and
c. initiate the preparation of the ECMP and ensure that the plan to be formulated
will be in accordance with the aforesaid JMC.

For all the observations noted we recommended the following: 
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) should create an Inventory Committee, consisting of a representative from the Office of the Municipal Mayor, General Services Office (GSO) and Accounting Office.

The GSO should maintain Property Cards per class and category of property, plant and equipment indicating acquisition date, the brief descriptions and work back on the previous years and reconcile with the records of the accounting office to determine the correctness of the property records.  Update the property issue slip/PAR to check custodianship of the property.

The LCE should require each department head to fast track the inventory taking of all properties under their office and submit the inventory report to the committee for validation and verification of the correctness and existence of the property.

In the event that an inventory committee is created it should check the condition of the property, any damage, obsolete and unserviceable property shall be reported in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable and Waste Material Report and immediately request for disposal.  Prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and furnish the accounting office for any adjustment.


The LCE should compel the inventory committee to fully accomplish the RPCPPE, indicating the actual count and complete details, and the results of the actual count shall be reconciled with the property and accounting records. All discrepancies between physical count and property records must be investigated, adjusted and reconciled immediately.  From thereon, perform periodic inventory of PPE and prepare report on the results thereof, in accordance with existing regulations.   

The Municipality should drop the unserviceable and obsolete properties items from the books following the process prescribed by the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in Local Government under COA Circular No. 92-386.

observations.recommendation:
In order to address the noted observations and increase collections, the Team recommended that management:

Formulate a clear cut policy (SB Ordinance) that will improve the assessment of business taxes such as:

Require all business establishments with at least gross receipts of over P500, 000.00 per annum that the sworn statement of gross sales or receipts for the preceding calendar year should be supported with documents such as audited financial statements and BIR Business Tax Returns (VAT & Percentage Tax Remittance).

For smaller firms and market, make use of presumptive minimum gross sales/receipts levels.

For lessors of Real Estate, sworn statements should at least be supported with notarized contracts of lease.

Require that all real estate transfer tax payments must be accompanied by confirmation of payment of other taxes, including the business tax and mayor’s permit fees.

Require the Municipal Treasurer to inspect business establishments within the jurisdiction of the Municipality to ensure effective and efficient collection of business taxes as mandated under [Section 470 (d) (4)] of LGC.  

Conduct and intensify business tax mapping. If possible, the Treasurer’s Office coordinates with the Planning Office by linking to the geographic information system (GIS) in order to identify new business and commercial areas more easily.

Sannguniang Bayan may pass an ordinance prescribing collection of eco-tourism fee for every visitor, similar or patterned to what is being imposed by LGU of El Nido, Palawan since 2011.
observations.managementAction: Follow up appeal to the SB to pass an ordinance regarding eco-tourism fee collection.
observations.reason: Lack of SB Ordinance to effect collection.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the immediate implementation of the Municipal Resolution imposing regulations for the use and operation of stalls/booths at Coron Commercial Complex. Local Chief Executive must enter into contract with the stallholders and management of the Coron Gateway Development Corporation in order to impose the payment of monthly rentals on the occupied/leased stalls.
observations.managementAction: Review of existing contracts are being conducted for renewal and issuance of contract with the stall holders.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management should require that all requests for the procurement of medicines be supported with a certification signed by the requisitioning officer that the medicines fall within and conform to PNDF, Volume 1, current edition. In addition, purchase of medicines not included in the PNDF shall be supported with written approval of the Head of the National Drug Policy Office, pursuant to the above regulations.

We have recommended that management establish a MSWMB and comply with the requirements and provisions set forth in RA 9003 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations, including the preparation of a MSWMP.
observations.reason: The LGU has already formulated a SWMB Plan but is still subject for approval by the appropriate government approving authority. However, sanitary landfill projects still remained unimplemented.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management should call for Accounting Office to effect adjustments on the erroneous recording of trust transactions. Require focal offices to implement PPAs timely and in accordance with the agreements set forth in the MOAs; and oblige the accounting and other concerned offices to submit, henceforth, fund utilization reports/liquidations complete with the supporting documentary requirements to the COA for audit purposes.
observations.reason: The LGU furnishes this office with utilization reports. However, it was observed that the balance of the account Due to NGAs account amounting to P50,722,265.30 still includes fund balances for projects which did not have any movement for the year.

observations.recommendation: In order to measure the responsiveness and effectiveness of the GAD activities and to determine whether these catered to the intents of RA 7192, the municipality] is advised to henceforth submit GAD accomplishment reports to DILG and other oversight bodies identified in the implementing rules and regulations.
